statement.markdown:
Given an array of integers and a target sum, validate if there exists a subset of the array that sums up to the target sum. The function `solve` is expected to return `true` if a valid subset is found, otherwise `false`.

      The function uses a backtracking approach to explore all possible combinations of the array.

      However, there is a logical bug in the code which needs to be fixed. The bug causes the function to return incorrect results for certain inputs.

      You are expected to identify the bug and fix it to make the function work correctly for all test cases.
statement.inputFormat: Input will consist of two lines. The first line will contain two space-separated integers, n (the size of the array) and the target sum. The second line will contain n space-separated integers representing the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: Output should be a boolean value indicating whether a valid subset is found.
statement.notes: The function is expected to run in a reasonable time for n = 1000. The array elements will be between 1 and 1000.

examples.explanation: A valid subset is {4, 3, 3} which sums up to 10.
No valid subset sums up to 15.

hints.text: Recall the concept of bitmask and how it can be used to represent a subset of an array.
The bug is likely related to the handling of the bitmask.

editorial.title: Permutation Matrix Validation Bug Fix
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a bitmask to keep track of the elements in the current subset.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can use a bitmask to keep track of the elements in the current subset. We can also use a recursive approach to explore all possible combinations of the array.

starterCode.javascript:
function solve(n, target, arr, index, sum, bitmask) {

}

functionSignature.cpp: bool solve(int n, int target, int arr[], int index, int sum, int bitmask);
functionSignature.java: boolean solve(int n, int target, int[] arr, int index, int sum, int bitmask);
functionSignature.python: def solve(self, n, target, nums: list[int], index: int, sum_: int, bitmask: int) -> bool:
functionSignature.javascript: var solve = function(n, target, arr, index, sum, bitmask);
